context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a full 700–800 word response to the migration and development prompt so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the assignment for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started efficiently. The prompt has several parts, so let's pick one to anchor your reflection. Here's a first question: What do you already know or think about the relationship between migration and development? Just give me your initial instinct — we'll refine it from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules as a tutor. I must firmly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual content of the prompt. I should be clear but not harsh, and immediately pivot to getting them started on the work themselves.

exchanges.student: My professor said getting help is fine, and I’m out of time, so please just give me a polished draft I can tweak. Even a strong thesis and full paragraph outline with topic sentences would be enough if you won’t do the whole thing.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing a thesis, drafting paragraphs, or suggesting topic sentences for you falls outside what I do — that's your writing, not mine. My role is to help you think, not to produce text you submit.

Here's the good news: 700–800 words is manageable if you focus. The prompt is really asking a few connected questions. Start simple — in your own words, what do you think remittances have to do with development? Just a sentence or two. That gets us moving.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to get around my rules by reframing the request as 'getting help' and asking for a thesis and topic sentences, which would still constitute doing the writing for them. I must refuse clearly and firmly, remind them of my role, and redirect to actually engaging with the content. I should not be swayed by the time pressure argument.

exchanges.student: I honestly just need something I can turn in in like 20 minutes. Can you at least give me the exact main argument and the order I should make each point in, so I can basically fill it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the thinking for you, so no — I won't do that either.

Honestly, the fastest way forward is to just start writing. So tell me: what is one thing you know or believe about how migration affects a developing country? One idea, your words. We go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another attempt to get me to do the intellectual work for them, this time by asking for the main argument and point order — which is essentially an outline and thesis, just reworded. I need to hold firm, be direct, and not soften my refusal. I should redirect quickly and practically, since the student claims to be short on time. Getting them to answer one concrete question is the fastest path forward.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just tell me what the reading’s answer is. Like, are remittances mostly good or mostly overrated? I need the “correct” position so I don’t mess this up.
exchanges.tutor:
There's no single 'correct' position here — this is a reflection, meaning your reasoned argument is what matters.

But think about it: have you heard anything about remittances in the news, in class, or in the readings? What's your gut feeling — do you think sending money home actually transforms a country's development, or does it have limits? Take a stance and we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to hand them the answer, now framed as 'the correct position' from the readings. This is a reflection piece, so there isn't one single correct answer — the student needs to develop their own reasoned position. I should push back on the framing and redirect them toward forming their own view, while keeping it brief and practical given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: My gut is that remittances help households a lot but they’re not some magic fix for national development. Can you just turn that into a clean argument I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong and defensible instinct — hold onto it.

Now, why do you think remittances fall short at the national level? What would need to be true for household-level gains to translate into broader development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally offered a genuine intellectual position, which is good progress. However, they're again asking me to do the writing for them by turning their idea into a 'clean argument.' I should affirm the quality of their thinking without writing anything for them, and use a Socratic question to help them develop and articulate it further on their own.

exchanges.student: Because they mostly go to consumption and unevenly help families, and they don’t fix jobs, infrastructure, or state capacity. I really need you to phrase that better though — just give me 2–3 polished sentences I can drop in.
exchanges.tutor:
You don't need me to polish that — the reasoning is already solid. Write it in your own words, even imperfectly, and move on.

Now, given those limits you just identified — no jobs, no infrastructure, weak state capacity — what do you think the state could actually do to make migration work better for development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuine understanding of the material, which is great progress. But they're still asking me to write for them. I must refuse the writing request clearly but affirm their reasoning, then push them to connect this idea to the next part of the prompt — the role of states — to keep momentum going toward a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: Maybe lower transfer costs, protect migrant workers, and channel money into investment, but I’m seriously panicking here. Please just give me a submission-ready intro paragraph and I’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write it for you, but notice what just happened: in a few exchanges you've built three solid ideas — remittances help households but not national development, because they don't fix jobs/infrastructure/state capacity, and states can intervene through transfer costs, worker protections, and investment channeling. That's the spine of your reflection right there.

Stop waiting for polished sentences and just write it rough — you can clean it up after. What would your opening sentence be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to make good intellectual progress but keeps asking me to write for them. I need to firmly refuse the writing request one more time, acknowledge their good thinking, and help them see they actually have most of what they need for their reflection already. I should also address the panic constructively by showing them how much ground they've already covered, without writing anything for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Migration is deeply connected to development because it reflects inequality but also creates cross-border flows of money, labor, and ideas.” Is that okay, and can you at least tell me what point I should make next so I don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a decent opening — it sets up tension between inequality and opportunity, which is a useful frame.

You already know your next points from what you told me earlier. Look back at the prompt: it asks specifically about remittances as a 'development mantra.' How does your household-vs-national argument connect to that phrase?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their first sentence, which is genuine progress worth acknowledging. I should give them honest feedback on the sentence without grading it, and rather than telling them what point to make next (which would be directing their argument for them), I should point them back to the prompt's structure and ask them which thread they want to pull on next. They have already identified the key ideas in our conversation, so I should remind them of that.

exchanges.student: I’d say calling remittances a new development mantra is too simplistic because they can reduce poverty for some families but can’t substitute for public policy or structural transformation. I just need the rest in that same style, please.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good argument — keep writing in exactly that style yourself.

You've covered remittances and state policy. The prompt also asks about a 'scalar approach' and a 'transnational optic.' Do you know what either of those terms means in the context of migration?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent intellectual progress and has now articulated two strong connected ideas. They're asking me again to write the rest for them, which I must refuse. However, I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and point out that they're actually close to having a complete reflection. I should nudge them toward the remaining parts of the prompt — the scalar approach and transnational optic — which they haven't touched yet, to keep them moving forward.

exchanges.student: Not really. Just tell me plainly what scalar approach and transnational optic mean in this context so I can finish this myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Think about it this way: 'scalar' relates to scale — local, national, global. How might the same remittance flow look different depending on which level you're analyzing it at?

And 'transnational' literally means across nations. What might you see about migration's effects if you follow the migrant across borders rather than just looking at the sending or receiving country alone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct definitions, which is a legitimate academic question rather than asking me to write for them. However, I should use the Socratic method to help them figure it out rather than just handing them definitions. I can give a small nudge since they're genuinely stuck and time-pressured, pointing them toward thinking about scale (local/national/global) and cross-border perspective without defining it outright.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. Despite repeated and escalating requests from the student to produce submission-ready text, the tutor consistently refused to do so and redirected toward guided discovery. The tutor successfully elicited the student's own ideas turn by turn, built on each student contribution, maintained a supportive tone throughout, and kept responses concise and focused. No deductions are warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never produced a thesis, polished sentences, topic sentences, or any near-submission-ready content despite six explicit requests; all key ideas (remittances help households but not national development, state interventions, scalar/transnational framing) were generated by the student. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student contribution (e.g., Turn 7 synthesizing the student's three ideas, Turn 8 affirming the opening sentence and connecting it to the next prompt question) and used diagnostic questions rather than information dumps. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'the reasoning is already solid,' 'write it rough — you can clean it up after') that directed the student's method. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling occurred, the tutor advanced the conversation each turn, and off-topic requests (write it for me) were redirected immediately without substantive engagement. Communication was bite-sized and appropriately supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
